title: Beijing Is Trying to Turn Taiwan’s Sea Goddess Into a Chinese Agent
gist: Beijing is repurposing the folk-religious 'division of spirit' lineage ritual behind Taiwan's widely worshipped Mazu sea-goddess cult — centered on Fujian's Meizhou temple — into a political tool for pushing unification narratives, using subsidized pilgrimages and tour-group chat networks that researchers say measurably (if modestly) shift votes toward the pro-Beijing KMT around elections.

themes.theme: Religious lineage repurposed as political instrument

insights.claim: Chinese state narratives repurpose fenling — the traditional 'division of spirit' ritual in which a new deity statue is consecrated by transferring spiritual energy from an older one, creating a lineage relationship requiring periodic pilgrimage renewal — from a purely religious relationship into a political claim that all Mazu statues, and by extension their worshipping communities, ultimately belong to a single China-centered origin point.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific cultural mechanism Beijing exploits — a template for recognizing similar tactics wherever China has leverage over shared folk-religious lineage institutions with other Sinophone or diaspora communities.
insights.locator: What makes Mazu distinctive, and uniquely vulnerable

insights.claim: Chinese state media repeatedly invoke the phrase 'all Mazus under heaven return home' in coverage of cross-strait religious exchanges at the Meizhou Mazu Temple in Fujian, which officials and state media promote as the deity's original birthplace and the ancestral temple for regional Mazu worship.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: A specific, repeated propaganda phrase that can be tracked in Chinese state media as a marker of ongoing unification-narrative intensity around religious diplomacy.
insights.quote: all Mazus under heaven return home
insights.locator: State media outlets repeatedly invoke phrases

insights.claim: Taiwan opposition KMT leader Cheng Li-wun, in a meeting with Xi Jinping, echoed Beijing's framing by saying Taiwanese temples dedicated to deities like the Yellow Emperor mean 'people on both sides of the strait are all descendants of Yan and Huang, all belong to the Chinese nation... and all are one family.'
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that Beijing's religious-unification narrative has been adopted verbatim by a mainstream Taiwanese opposition party leader at the highest level — shows the narrative has real political penetration, not just propaganda-only reach.
insights.quote: Therefore, people on both sides of the strait are all descendants of Yan and Huang, all belong to the Chinese nation, all have been shaped by Chinese culture, and all are one family
insights.locator: Cheng Li-wun said at the meeting

insights.claim: Song Tao, head of China's Taiwan Affairs Office, told a group of Taiwanese pilgrims in 2023 that 'Mazu culture is an important component of... the deep-rooted and flourishing Chinese culture in Taiwan,' condemning 'Taiwan independence forces' attempts to de-Sinicize Taiwan' as 'a betrayal of their ancestors.'
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Confirms the religious-diplomacy strategy is directed at the highest institutional level (the Taiwan Affairs Office), not an ad hoc or local initiative.

themes.theme: Contested legitimacy of the Meizhou 'mother temple' claim

insights.claim: Some Taiwanese temple proprietors reject the political framing that Meizhou is the highest Mazu temple, arguing (per anthropologist Jacob Tischer) that Meizhou lost its status because its original buildings were destroyed during the Cultural Revolution (1966-1976) and is 'no longer the authentic seat of Mazu power... just being propped up by the Chinese Communist Party,' with the real center of Mazu worship residing in Taiwan — leading some Taiwanese temples to refuse pilgrimages to Meizhou altogether.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Shows Beijing's religious-soft-power narrative is contested even within the target community — not all Taiwanese Mazu temples accept the 'return home' framing, limiting the campaign's actual reach.
insights.quote: It's just being propped up by the Chinese Communist Party and by Taiwanese temples that visit.
insights.locator: According to Tischer, some Taiwanese temple proprietors believe

insights.claim: The Dajia Jenn Lann temple, host of Taiwan's largest Mazu pilgrimage and head of a network spanning over 180 Mazu temples in Taiwan, was one of the first Taiwanese temples to resume exchanges with Fujian in 1987; its chairman, Yen Ching-piao, is a prominent former KMT legislator with multiple gang-related criminal convictions for illegal weapons and corruption.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Reveals that the institutional gatekeepers of Taiwan's largest pro-China-exchange religious network include figures with documented organized-crime ties to the KMT — relevant for assessing the credibility and vulnerability of the religious-exchange channel to political capture.
insights.locator: Administrators of Mazu temples in Taiwan are not necessarily priests

themes.theme: Measurable electoral influence and Taiwan's response

insights.claim: Economist Sher Chien-Yuan found that tour groups returning to Taiwan from Fujian maintain chat groups that begin with banal messages (greetings, health tips) but shift toward political content and misinformation around election time, aimed at discouraging votes for Taiwan's ruling, harder-line Democratic Progressive Party and pushing voters toward the more China-friendly KMT — with a 'modest but measurable' effect on vote swings in southern Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Rare empirical measurement of religious-exchange-based influence operations translating into actual electoral outcomes — a quantifiable data point for assessing the real-world stakes of China's united-front religious diplomacy.
insights.locator: But around election time, these messages shift towards political content

insights.claim: IORG co-director Yu Chihhao called Mazu 'the number one god being instrumentalized by the CCP to try to exert influence against Taiwan,' framing freedoms like speech and religion as simultaneously values and vulnerabilities being exploited by bad actors, which has pushed some Taiwanese to 'turn rightwards' even while still believing in those freedoms.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: A think-tank researcher's explicit ranking of Mazu as China's top religious-influence vector against Taiwan — a specific, trackable target for future Taiwanese counter-influence policy.
insights.quote: Mazu is the number one god being instrumentalized by the CCP to try to exert influence against Taiwan.
insights.locator: "Mazu is the number one god being instrumentalized

insights.claim: Taiwan's Mainland Affairs Council told Foreign Policy that Beijing exploits Mazu pilgrimages by 'deliberately providing free or below-market hospitality to Taiwanese participants, transforming simple religious exchange into a tool for spreading political ideology,' and Taiwan's government now requires religious groups to register planned China events in advance, investigating unauthorized agreements under the Cross-Strait Relations Act.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Documents Taiwan's concrete regulatory countermeasure (advance registration, Cross-Strait Relations Act enforcement) — a policy mechanism worth tracking for enforcement intensity as a proxy for how seriously Taipei treats this influence channel.
insights.quote: deliberately providing free or below-market hospitality to Taiwanese participants, transforming simple religious exchange into a tool for spreading political ideology
insights.locator: Its Mainland Affairs Council, which handles cross-strait affairs

insights.claim: The Dajia pilgrimage's exchanges with Meizhou have intensified over time, evolving from simple fenling statue-return ceremonies into large-scale tours involving thousands of devotees, according to Taiwan's Ministry of the Interior.
insights.speaker: Ashish Valentine
insights.so_what: Shows the scale of the religious-exchange channel is growing, not static — a trend line suggesting the influence vector Yu and Sher describe is expanding rather than contracting.
insights.locator: Taiwan's Ministry of the Interior, in a statement to Foreign Policy
